What People Do Wrong: Calling .unwrap() on Result and Option throughout production code
Why Its Bad: Panics at runtime on any failure; crashes the entire program; no error recovery possible
Better Approach: Use ? operator for propagation; use .unwrap_or_else() or .unwrap_or_default() for defaults; use .expect() with context when panic is intentional

What People Do Wrong: Cloning values unnecessarily to avoid borrow checker errors instead of restructuring code
Why Its Bad: Wastes memory and CPU on heap allocations; hides design problems; often indicates misunderstanding of lifetimes
Better Approach: Restructure code to use references; reduce lock scope; use scoped borrows; split struct methods to avoid conflicting borrows

What People Do Wrong: Using String where specific types (enums, newtypes, or structs) would provide safety
Why Its Bad: No compile-time validation; typos compile but fail at runtime; impossible to enumerate valid values
Better Approach: Use enums for known sets; newtypes for validated values; &str for borrowed text

What People Do Wrong: Calling blocking operations (std::fs, thread::sleep, CPU-heavy code) inside async functions
Why Its Bad: Blocks the Tokio worker thread; starves other tasks; reduces throughput; may cause deadlocks
Better Approach: Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking for blocking work; use tokio::fs for async file ops; use tokio::time::sleep for delays

What People Do Wrong: Silencing compiler warnings with #[allow(...)] instead of fixing the underlying issue
Why Its Bad: Masks real bugs; dead code accumulates; unused imports slow compilation; ignored warnings reduce trust in CI
Better Approach: Fix warnings at the source; configure #[deny(warnings)] in CI; use #[allow] only for documented reasons

What People Do Wrong: Marking fields or types as pub when they should be private, exposing internals to consumers
Why Its Bad: Callers depend on implementation details; cannot change internals without breaking changes; unsafe direct access
Better Approach: Use private fields with getter methods; expose only what consumers need; use pub(crate) for internal visibility

Keywords: monolith, large struct, single responsibility, bloat
What People Do Wrong: Putting too many fields and methods into a single struct
Why Its Bad: Hard to test; hard to understand; hard to extend; violates single responsibility; merge conflicts in teams
Better Approach: Split into focused structs; use composition; extract related fields into sub-structs

What People Do Wrong: Wrapping everything in Arc<Mutex<T>> as the default concurrency strategy
Why Its Bad: High contention; reduces parallelism to serial access; hidden deadlocks; complex API
Better Approach: Use channels for message passing; use DashMap for concurrent maps; use atomic types for counters; use actors for stateful components

What People Do Wrong: Not running cargo clippy or ignoring its suggestions
Why Its Bad: Misses common mistakes; ships suboptimal code; does not catch idiomatic violations
Better Approach: Run cargo clippy in CI with -D warnings; configure clippy.toml for project-specific rules
Code Good:
# In CI:
cargo clippy -- -D warnings

# With extra lints:
cargo clippy -- -W clippy::pedantic -D warnings

# Project config in clippy.toml:
msrv = "1.75.0"

What People Do Wrong: Optimizing code before measuring; using unsafe for speed without profiling; sacrificing readability
Why Its Bad: Optimizes the wrong thing; makes code harder to maintain; unsafe code introduces soundness risks; benchmarks may not show improvement
Better Approach: Profile first with cargo flamegraph; benchmark with criterion; optimize only hot paths

What People Do Wrong: Adding explicit lifetimes everywhere when the code could be restructured to avoid them
Why Its Bad: Hard to read; hard to maintain; discourages contributions; often indicates a design problem
Better Approach: Restructure to use owned data; use Arc/Rc for shared ownership; use Cow for sometimes-borrowed; let lifetime elision work

What People Do Wrong: Using _ wildcard to catch all error variants without handling them properly
Why Its Bad: Silent failures; missing new error variants; hard to debug; logs show generic messages
Better Approach: Handle each variant explicitly; log specific error information; use non_exhaustive to force handling

What People Do Wrong: Using println! or eprintln! for application logging instead of a structured logging framework
Why Its Bad: No log levels; no structured fields; no filtering; cannot be disabled; goes to stdout not log system
Better Approach: Use the tracing crate for structured, leveled, filterable logging

Anti-Pattern: Not Implementing Error for Custom Errors
What People Do Wrong: Creating error types without implementing std::error::Error and Display
Why Its Bad: Cannot use ? operator; cannot use anyhow::Error; error chain is broken; cannot downcast
Better Approach: Derive Error with thiserror; or manually implement Display and Error

What People Do Wrong: Using Rc<RefCell<T>> everywhere to avoid thinking about ownership
Why Its Bad: Runtime borrow checking (can panic); performance overhead; not thread-safe; defeats Rust's compile-time guarantees
Better Approach: Restructure ownership; use indices into a Vec instead of Rc; use ECS pattern for complex relationships

What People Do Wrong: Representing invariants through runtime checks instead of the type system
Why Its Bad: Invariants can be violated; runtime errors instead of compile-time errors; defensive checks scattered everywhere
Better Approach: Use newtypes for validated values; type-state for state machines; enums for closed sets; phantom types for compile-time tags

What People Do Wrong: Deriving PartialEq without Eq for types that are actually totally ordered, especially when using HashMap/HashSet
Why Its Bad: Types used as HashMap keys require Eq (not just PartialEq); missing Eq means the compiler cannot guarantee reflexivity
Better Approach: Derive Eq alongside PartialEq for types with total equality; impl Eq is free when PartialEq is derived

What People Do Wrong: Using unsafe blocks without clear documentation of safety invariants
Why Its Bad: Potential undefined behavior; memory safety violations; data races; crashes in production
Better Approach: Document safety invariants as comments; minimize unsafe scope; create safe abstractions over unsafe code; use miri to check
